summary: Big day in the SE BeltLine corridor as Reynoldstown construction finally gets moving near Memorial and the Eastside Trail. Meanwhile, Decatur's dropping that affordable housing at Cottages at Midway, and Vela Park's food hall is opening next month in East Lake.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Development Finally Breaks Ground After Five-Year Wait
topStory.summary: That empty lot on Chester Avenue near Memorial Drive is finally seeing action. After sitting cleared for nearly five years, the 85-unit townhomes and condos are starting construction just a block from the Eastside Trail. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers two years ago and is moving forward with what locals will know as Camber Crossing.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the Eastside Trail lately, you've probably wondered what's happening with that cleared lot on Chester Avenue where it meets Fulton Terrace. Good news, Reynoldstown friends: after sitting empty for nearly five years, construction is finally underway on what's going to be called Camber Crossing.

The site has quite the story. Toll Brothers cleared the original buildings back in 2021 with big plans for 85 residential units, but then... nothing. Two years ago, Atlanta-based Embry Development stepped in and bought the 2-acre site, inheriting all the existing permits and approvals. Now they're moving forward with a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos just a block west of our beloved Eastside Trail.

This is exactly the kind of missing middle housing Reynoldstown needs as the neighborhood continues to evolve. The location couldn't be better, walking distance to both the BeltLine action and Memorial Drive's growing restaurant scene. Embry Development managing partner confirmed they're keeping the original vision alive while adding their own touches. It's a relief to see movement on a corner that's been sitting there looking lonely for way too long.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's Cottages at Midway Offers Missing Middle Housing Alternative
stories.summary: With Decatur home prices hitting $700K median, this 18-house village on Midway Road is trying something different. The pocket neighborhood features standalone houses around shared courtyards, designed as a more affordable entry point to ITP living. It's replacing two vacant lots with what developers hope will be accessible single-family options.
stories.body:
Anyone house hunting in Decatur lately knows the sticker shock is real. With median home values climbing past $700,000, Cottages at Midway is attempting to offer a different path into one of our most beloved ITP communities. This isn't your typical subdivision, it's 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace on just over 2 acres.

The pocket neighborhood sits on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur, replacing two underused lots that have been sitting there waiting for the right project. What makes this interesting is the village-style approach. Instead of cramming as many units as possible onto the land, they're creating a community feel with shared outdoor spaces while still giving everyone their own front door.

Construction is moving along and they're planning a soft launch next week to start drumming up interest. The big question, of course, is whether 'relatively attainable' will actually translate to prices that middle-income families can handle. In today's market, even the missing middle can feel pretty expensive. But credit to the developers for at least trying a different approach to the housing puzzle in one of Atlanta's most desirable school districts.
stories.imageAlt: Site map of Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: Ackerman & Co.'s townhome project on Hopkins Street is finally rising up after a year of prep work. The development, now called GW Parkside, will bring 23 for-sale units to the West End BeltLine corridor. The first seven properties should hit the market this spring, adding rare homeownership options to the southwest trail.
stories.body:
Remember when Ackerman & Co. started tearing things down on Hopkins Street near the West End BeltLine section last year? Well, those townhomes are finally starting to show themselves. What was once called Hopkins and White Townhomes has been rebranded as Gordon White Parkside (or GW Parkside if you're into the short version).

This is the same developer behind Lee + White, so they know the West End market pretty well. The 23-unit project is bringing something that's been pretty rare along the southwest BeltLine: new for-sale housing. Most of what we've seen in this area has been rental apartments and mixed-use developments, so having townhomes you can actually buy is a nice change.

The first seven units are getting close to completion and should be available this spring according to the signage on site. It's exactly the kind of development the West End needs as the BeltLine brings more foot traffic and energy to this part of town. Plus, being right there on the trail means you can walk to Lee + White for dinner or hop on your bike to reach downtown. That's the kind of connectivity that makes these neighborhoods so special.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens Next Month
stories.summary: The mastermind behind NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to East Lake. Vela Park Food Works will debut in mid-April with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. It's part of the new Vela Park apartment community near East Lake Golf Club.
stories.body:
Here's some exciting news for eastside food lovers: Robert Montwaid, the guy who created New York's legendary Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food hall next month. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction now and should be welcoming hungry folks by mid-April.

This isn't just another food court situation. Montwaid has partnered with the developers of the new Vela Park apartment complex near East Lake Golf Club to create six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor seating. If his track record with previous Atlanta projects is any indication, this is going to be something special.

The location makes perfect sense for the eastside's growing dining scene. You've got all the East Lake and Kirkwood residents nearby, plus it's easily accessible from the BeltLine corridor. While we don't have details yet on which chefs are involved, Montwaid has a knack for curating interesting concepts that go way beyond typical food hall fare. Mark your calendars for mid-April and get ready for another reason to explore that part of town.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The pop-up-turned-permanent spot serves soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. It's the culmination of years working in Atlanta's restaurant scene and running successful pop-ups.
stories.body:
There's a new spot to check out in Inman Park that deserves your attention. Mushi Ni officially opened today on Elizabeth Street, and it represents a big milestone for chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, who are opening their first brick-and-mortar restaurant after years of pop-ups and working in other people's kitchens.

Both chefs have serious credentials. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and together they've been building a following with their Asian-American comfort food concept. The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25.

The location is pretty perfect too, right off the Freedom Park Trail so you can easily walk or bike over from Virginia-Highland, Poncey-Highland, or deeper into Inman Park. They're open for lunch and dinner, and the cocktail program includes things like Thai tea martinis that sound absolutely dangerous in the best possible way. It's exciting to see talented chefs finally get their own space to do their thing.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Opens with Austin Flair Near the BeltLine
stories.summary: The Austin pitmaster brings his acclaimed barbecue to Ansley Mall, complete with a stunning upstairs smokehouse overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive smokers run 24/7 to produce what might be Atlanta's best brisket. The location behind The Cook's Warehouse puts serious Texas barbecue within walking distance of the trail.
stories.body:
Get ready for some serious barbecue, Atlanta. Lewis Barbecue has opened at Ansley Mall, and holy smokes (literally), this place is doing something special. Pitmaster John Lewis, who helped put Austin barbecue on the map, has brought his operation to the space behind The Cook's Warehouse, and the setup is unlike anything I've seen in Atlanta.

The real showstopper is upstairs in what Lewis calls the smokehouse. Instead of hiding their cookers in some back room, they've put six 22-foot smokers in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. These custom-built behemoths run 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts, cranking out what might be the city's best brisket.

Lewis helped weld these smokers himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and the attention to detail shows in every bite. The location couldn't be better for BeltLine walkers and Midtown folks looking for authentic Texas barbecue. This isn't some corporate chain trying to fake their way through barbecue - this is the real deal from a pitmaster who knows his craft. The walk from the BeltLine is going to become a regular pilgrimage for a lot of us.

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Nearly Finished
stories.summary: After nearly a decade in planning, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a car-centric drag strip into a people-friendly Complete Street is almost done. The project includes protected bike lanes, improved sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to the Atlanta city limits. It's a model for how to redesign streets for everyone.
stories.body:
If you've driven or walked along West Howard Avenue in Decatur lately, you've probably noticed the major transformation happening. What was once a high-speed corridor that felt more like a highway than a neighborhood street is becoming something much more civilized, and the project is finally nearing completion.

This Complete Streets makeover has been nearly a decade in the making, stretching roughly one mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to Paden Circle at the Atlanta city limits. The centerpiece is a two-way protected cycle track with concrete barriers that gives cyclists a safe route separate from car traffic. But it's not just about bikes - they've also upgraded sidewalks, added traffic calming measures, and generally made the whole corridor more human-scaled.

What I love about this project is that it shows how you can retrofit a car-centric street without completely starting over. West Howard was basically a drag strip before, the kind of road where people would speed through without thinking twice about pedestrians or cyclists. Now it's becoming the kind of street that actually serves the neighborhood rather than just moving cars through it as fast as possible. Other cities should take notes on how Decatur pulled this off.
stories.imageAlt: Protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Fight Heats Up
stories.summary: City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities and require documentation when federal agents work with Atlanta police. The meeting also saw dozens of residents criticize Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' first-ever union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
There was a lot happening at yesterday's City Council Public Safety Committee meeting, and most of it involved people being pretty frustrated with various levels of government. The committee unanimously advanced two resolutions aimed at controlling Immigration and Customs Enforcement activities in Atlanta - one banning ICE detention facilities within city limits and another requiring APD to document when federal agents are involved in their operations.

But the bigger drama was around the firefighters' union contract. Dozens of Atlanta residents showed up specifically to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighters' union, which the council already ratified back in April 2025. It's a weird situation where the legislative branch did their job, but the executive branch is dragging their feet.

Both ICE resolutions head to the full council for a vote on April 20th, and they'll likely pass given the unanimous committee support. As for the fire union contract, this feels like it's becoming a bigger political problem for the mayor than it needs to be. When dozens of people are showing up to city meetings to complain about your handling of public safety workers' contracts, that's not great optics for an administration that's already dealing with airport chaos and other federal headaches.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking at podium

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Land in National Top 10 Rankings
stories.summary: A national ranking website just named Atlantic Station the #1 best place to live in America, with Midtown coming in at #6. The rankings looked at public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across 18,000+ neighborhoods nationwide. Not bad for a city that gets called overpriced and overhyped.
stories.body:
Well, this is interesting. Niche.com just dropped their '2026 Best Places to Live in America' rankings, and Atlanta claimed two spots in the top 10. Atlantic Station grabbed the #1 spot nationally, while Midtown landed at #6. That's 20% of the top 10, which isn't too shabby for a city that gets plenty of criticism.

The rankings considered public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Say what you will about the methodology, but Atlantic Station's mix of walkability, transit access, and urban amenities clearly resonated with their algorithm. Midtown's high ranking makes total sense too - it's got the cultural attractions, restaurants, transit, and that urban energy people are looking for.

Of course, anyone actually living in these neighborhoods might have some thoughts about the 'cost of living' factor in these rankings. Both areas have gotten pretty expensive, especially Atlantic Station with all those high-end apartments. But credit where it's due: these neighborhoods offer something a lot of American cities struggle with, which is dense, walkable, transit-connected urban living. Maybe we're doing something right after all.

stories.headline: Deadly Shootings Hit Vine City and MLK Drive
stories.summary: Two separate shootings within a mile of each other left one man dead and another person hospitalized Tuesday night. The fatal shooting happened outside a Vine City food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard, while a second shooting on MLK Drive injured one person and a child was hurt by debris. No arrests announced yet.
stories.body:
Tuesday night was rough in northwest Atlanta, with two shootings happening within about a mile of each other. The more serious incident happened just before 9 p.m. outside a food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard near Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard in Vine City. A man was shot and rushed to the hospital, where he later died.

About 30 minutes later, there was another shooting on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive SW near a barber shop. One person was shot and drove themselves to the hospital (they're stable), and a child was injured by debris but thankfully not seriously hurt. The barber shop's windows were completely shot out, with glass scattered all over the sidewalk.

Atlanta police haven't connected these two incidents or released any information about suspects or motives. It's unclear if anyone was specifically targeted or if these were random acts of violence. What's frustrating is how close these happened to each other in both time and location - it really rattles a community when this kind of violence clusters like that. Hopefully APD can get some answers soon and get these shooters off the streets.
stories.imageAlt: Crime scene tape at shooting location in Vine City

stories.headline: Eight-Foot Python Found Slithering on the BeltLine
stories.summary: Someone spotted a massive snake on the BeltLine and caught it on video, but wildlife officials are warning people not to handle unfamiliar animals. The python was reportedly 8-10 feet long and definitely not native to Georgia. A man in the video can be seen grabbing the snake, though it's unclear if he was the owner or just a brave bystander.
stories.body:
Well, here's something you don't see every day on the BeltLine. Joseph Ragland was walking the trail when he came across what he describes as an 8- to 10-foot python just hanging out in the bushes. And because this is 2026, of course he got it on video.

'That's not a typical Georgia snake; that's something different,' Ragland told reporters, which is probably the understatement of the week. In the video, you can see some guy just casually yanking this massive snake out of the vegetation like it's no big deal. 'Somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush,' Ragland explained, and honestly, that's the most Atlanta thing I've heard in a while.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue says the video is concerning because handling unknown snakes is dangerous, even if this particular python likely wasn't venomous. Nobody knows if the man in the video was the owner or just someone with questionable decision-making skills. Either way, if you're out on the BeltLine and see any large reptiles that don't belong in Georgia, maybe call the professionals instead of channeling your inner Steve Irwin.
stories.imageAlt: Man handling large python on Atlanta BeltLine
